episodes.title: Episode 7: Why They Came: Hope and the Black Community
episodes.description: In this episode, host and Jonestown survivor Leslie Wagner Wilson sits down with Sikivu Hutchinson to explore what drew so many Black families to the San Francisco area during the 1970s — and the deep sense of hope that the Peoples Temple represented for the Black community.Together, Leslie and Sikivu unpack the social and political climate of the era: the search for racial equality, economic opportunity, and genuine community in a country that had so often denied all three. They examine why the Temple's message of integration, dignity, and collective purpose resonated so powerfully with Black Americans — and how that hope was ultimately betrayed.This is a conversation about faith, longing, belonging, and the dreams that brought a generation to the Bay.🎙️ Host: Leslie Wagner Wilson, Jonestown survivor and author🎙️ Guest: Sikivu Hutchinson, author and scholarSikivu Hutchinson, PhD. is an educator, writer, and stage director with a background in gender and social justice youth leadership, professional development and training, as well as research on culturally responsive teaching, Black feminism, women of color feminism, sexual violence, LGBTQ+ youth and family affirming best practices, humanism and atheism. As the founder of the Women’s Leadership Project and Young Male Scholars’ programs she has successfully assisted first generation, foster care, undocumented, and LGBTQ students of color go on to college and careers. Show Notes and Linkshttps://www.amazon.com/White-Nights-P...https://thehumanist.com/magazine/wint...https://www.amazon.com/Humanists-Hood...Womens Leadership Projecthttp://www.sikivuhutchinson.comhttps://hollywoodprogressive.com/stag...https://www.huffpost.com/entry/jonest... #BlackJonestown #PeoplesTemple #JimJones #Jonestown #JonestownSurvivor #LeslieWagnerWilson #SikivuHutchinson #BlackHistory #BlackCommunity #SanFrancisco #BayArea #CivilRights #BlackChurch #Cult #CultSurvivor #AmericanHistory #blackvoicesmatter Subscribe and turn on notifications so you never miss an episode of Black Jonestown.

episodes.title: Episode 6: The Man with the File- Inside the Peoples Temple Investigation
episodes.description:
Before Jonestown became a tragedy the world could no longer ignore, someone was already watching. In this episode, host and Jonestown survivor Leslie Wagner Wilson sits down with David Reuben, Special Investigator with the San Francisco District Attorney's Office. 

David Reuben shares how he became involved in the investigation, what drew law enforcement's attention to Jim Jones and his organization, and what the evidence revealed about the Temple's activities. He takes us inside the findings — the files, the witnesses, and the disturbing picture that began to emerge. He reflects on the aftermath — what happened to the investigation, the evidence, and the people connected to it. This is the side of the Jonestown story that rarely gets told — the paper trail that existed before the tragedy, and the man who followed it.

Show Notes:
Church Vs. Statehttps://firstamendment.mtsu.edu/artic...New West Article/Linda Mertle Abusehttps://jonestown.sdsu.edu/wp-content...Affidavits & Lettershttps://jonestown.sdsu.edu/?page_id=8...

episodes.title: Episode 5: The Grift: Jack Colbert - Life Inside Peoples Temple
episodes.description:
In this powerful episode, survivor and Host Leslie Wagner Wilson sits down with Jack Colbert, a former member of People's Temple, for an intimate and unflinching conversation about what it meant to grow up inside of a movement that weaponized hope against the very people it claimed to serve. 
Jack opens up about his childhood within the Temple — the community, the faith, and the sense of belonging that drew so many families in. He reflects on what daily life looked like under Jim Jones, the control, the contradictions, and the moments that slowly began to crack the foundation of everything he believed. 
This is more than a story about a cult. It's a story about community, survival, and the complicated search for something real.

episodes.title: Episode 4: The Grift; Power, Money and the Promise
episodes.description:
In this episode of Black Jonestown, we trace the ideological blueprint that shaped Jim Jones long before Jonestown existed. Episode 4 examines Jones’s formative influences—specifically his ties to William Branham, a controversial revivalist whose theology, healing theatrics, and authoritarian control methods helped model how spiritual authority could be performed, protected, and monetized. 


We then widen the lens to Father Divine and the Peace Mission Movement, whose structure demonstrated something even more powerful than charisma: infrastructure. Through Father Divine, Jones witnessed how religious devotion could be fused with: Economic systemsCommunal livingLand ownershipPolitical accessCarefully managed racial opticsThis episode explores how Jones did not invent his methods—but studied them. Adapted them. And ultimately weaponized them. The Grift: Part 1 reveals how belief becomes currency, how loyalty becomes labor, and how power disguises itself as promise.This is not the story of a madman. This is the story of a student.

episodes.title: PREVIEW - Episode 6
episodes.description:
Every investigation has a paper trail. In our next episode, host and Jonestown survivor Leslie Wagner Wilson sits down with David Reuben, Special Investigator with the San Francisco District Attorney's Office. What did the files reveal? And what did law enforcement uncover? Episode 6 is coming, and this one goes deep inside the investigation. Stay tuned!

#BlackJonestown #PeoplesTemple #JimJones #Jonestown #LeslieWagnerWilson #DavidReuben #Investigation #Cults

episodes.title: Black Jonestown Update
episodes.description: Greetings, just a short video from me to you.

episodes.title: Honoring the Elders of Jonestown | A Tribute to Our Ancestors
episodes.description:
This tribute is dedicated to the senior citizens who lost their lives in Jonestown—elders, parents, grandparents, caregivers, teachers, and community pillars whose stories are too often overlooked.

Before Jonestown, they lived full lives.
They loved. They worked. They nurtured families.
They carried wisdom, culture, faith, and resilience.

In this moment of remembrance, we pause to honor them not as statistics or footnotes, but as ancestors—people who mattered, whose lives deserve dignity, reflection, and respect.

Each image you see represents a life lived, a voice once heard, and a legacy that remains.
We remember them.
We speak their names.
We hold space for their memory.

May this tribute serve as a moment of quiet reflection, respect, and remembrance.

Rest in peace, our ancestors. You are remembered.

🕊️
Black Jonestown

#Jonestown #JonestownMemorial #JimJones #BlackHistory #PeoplesTemple

episodes.title: Episode 3: Janet Shular: A Survivor’s Voice | Honoring Deanna Wilkinson | Black Jonestown
episodes.description:
Episode 3 of Black Jonestown centers the voice of Janet Shular, the oldest known surviving former member of People’s Temple, as she reflects on her life, her time within the movement, and the experiences that shaped her understanding of faith, community, and survival.

This episode also takes a moment to honor the life and legacy of Deanna Wilkinson — a gifted musician whose voice brought light, beauty, and joy to many. Deanna’s voice remains a powerful reminder that within a complicated and tragic history were real people, real gifts, and lives that deserve to be remembered!

#BlackJonestown  #PeoplesTemple #JonestownHistory #SurvivorStories
#BlackHistory

episodes.title: Episode 3 Preview | Janet Shular’s Story | Black Jonestown
episodes.description:
In Episode 3 of Black Jonestown, we sit down with Janet Shuler, the oldest surviving member of Peoples Temple, as she shares her journey in her own words.

An educated, accomplished professional in early childhood education, Janet joined Peoples Temple believing it was a place where her gifts could serve children and build community. And for a time, it was. But as the cracks began to show, Janet realized Jim Jones was not the man he claimed to be. What followed was a quiet awakening — and a carefully planned escape that saved her life and the lives of her family. This episode challenges the long-standing stereotypes about who followed Jim Jones. Janet Shuler was not ignorant or helpless. She was thoughtful, perceptive, and courageous — a woman who chose truth over fear.

We also honor the life and legacy of Deanna Wilkinson, one of Peoples Temple’s most extraordinary voices. Her music brought joy to many, and her story reminds us that behind every headline were real people with real gifts and real lives.

Episode 3 premieres December 17th.
Stay tuned.

episodes.title: Episode 2: Her Mother, Her Grandmother, Her Truth — Remembering Elsie Bell
episodes.description:
In Episode 2 of Black Jonestown, Leslie Wagner Wilson sits down with Nancy Shaw — a woman whose family was forever changed by the promises and deceptions of Jim Jones.

Nancy opens her heart about losing her mother, who died believing Jones would heal her, and her grandmother, who perished in Jonestown. Through honesty, pain, and clarity, she brings forward a story few have ever heard directly from those who lived it.

This conversation is raw, intimate, and deeply human.
It speaks to faith, manipulation, grief, and the resilience it takes to reclaim your narrative after unimaginable loss.

Following the interview, we honor the life of Elsie Bell, remembering her not just as a victim of Jonestown, but as a woman whose life, hope, and humanity mattered.

Black Jonestown is dedicated to telling the stories behind the headlines — to show the family, not the spectacle.

If this episode moved you, please like, comment, and subscribe so we can continue sharing the voices and stories that history tried to silence.

episodes.title: Why “Drinking the Kool-Aid” Isn’t a Joke — Leslie Wagner Wilson Speaks
episodes.description:
Survivor. Mother. Advocate. Truth-teller.

In this powerful TikTok message, Leslie Wagner Wilson, one of the survivors of the Jonestown tragedy, explains why the phrase “drinking the Kool-Aid” is deeply painful and harmful to those who lived through the event — and to the families whose loved ones never came home.

This clip is shared to bring understanding, context, and respect to a phrase that has been used casually for decades without acknowledging the real people behind it.

As we continue the mission of the Black Jonestown project — honoring the lives, the stories, and the humanity of those who were part of Peoples Temple — let this serve as a reminder:

Words matter. History matters. And so do the people who survived it.

episodes.title: Episode 2 Preview — Black Jonestown
episodes.description:
In the upcoming episode, we sit down with Nancy Shaw, a former child in Peoples Temple whose grandmother died in Jonestown. Nancy shares her story with real, raw, and emotional transparency as she reflects on the memories she’s carried for decades.

We also honor the lives of several Peoples Temple members who died in Jonestown. Their stories will be told through narration, shared pictures, and video—giving space to their truth, their humanity, and their legacy.

And for everyone who’s been reaching out… we hear you!!
Leslie Wagner Wilson will begin answering your questions in upcoming episodes.

If you have a question about Peoples Temple, Jonestown, or anything discussed so far, send it to:
📩 therealblackjonestown@gmail.com

Episode 2 drops Wednesday, December 3rd.
Thank you for supporting this work, honoring these lives, and helping ensure their stories are told with the care their legacy deserves.

#BlackJonestown

episodes.title: Black Jonestown Podcast Episode 1
episodes.description:
Episode 1 opens the Black Jonestown Podcast with two powerful voices who carry firsthand memories of Peoples Temple. Survivor Leslie Wagner Wilson shares her lived experience and the path that led her to become the executive producer of this series. We are also joined by Jack Colbert, who grew up inside Peoples Temple and offers a rare perspective from someone who witnessed the community from childhood.

Together, they reflect, remember, and speak openly about life before Jonestown and the moments that shaped everything. This conversation sets the foundation for the journey ahead.

Subscribe for more interviews, stories, and insights as we uncover the truth—through the people who lived it.

episodes.title: Black Jonestown Trailer
episodes.description:
They weren’t lost souls or blind followers!
They were mothers, fathers, children, and dreamers — people of faith, community, and purpose who believed in building a better world.

Black Jonestown is a cinematic tribute to the Black members of the People’s Temple — those who lived, those who died, and those who survived the tragedy in Jonestown, Guyana. For decades, their humanity has been overshadowed by headlines and myths. This story reclaims their truth.

Through powerful storytelling, archival imagery, and haunting narration, the Black Jonestown series reveals who they really were — not victims of madness, but believers in hope, equality, and belonging.

This is not about how they died.
It’s about how they lived — and why their voices still matter.

✨ The first episode premieres November 18, 2025!!

Subscribe, share, and remember — because their story deserves to be told.
